content:
I have "github/ocpn-manuals" locally only. It has regularly updated html documentation in "github/ocpn-manuals/docs"
should I push that up to my remote repository so that Mike can access it somehow?

Or is it better to have the "watchdog_doc" repository that can be named as a unique sub-module in Mikes ocpn-user-plugin-manual?

Submodule is too static and not the right thing to use. points to a certain commit.

A git submodule is a record within a host git repository that points to a specific commit in another external repository. Submodules are very static and only track specific commits. Submodules do not track git refs or branches and are not automatically updated when the host repository is updated. When adding a submodule to a repository a new .gitmodules file will be created.

see https://www.atlassian.com/git/tutorials/git-submodule

Don't get into submodules now. Frankly, you know too little about git yet for that.

You should (of course) push your changes on ocpn-manuals to github to make them visible. Probably not much idea to merge things yet (here, I'm upstream)

I think the right way to do this is to have an action script that runs in ocpn-user-plugin-manual that git fetches all of the plugin_doc html files into different directories. The ocpn-user-plugin-manual should then become a <username>>github.io public Github Page

I realized later that the html is being created in build/site and is not pushed up to the remote repository, so unless that is changed, the output cannot be done in a distributed fashion. I expect that Mike has decided to use antora/asciidoc to create a centralized version using the oplaydo opensource repository which will be something like oplaydo.github.io

Rick:
I am building the demo plugins site in exactly the same way as Alec. I do a local test with .gitignore docs and then push the result to Github. This automatically populates the github.io pages.
Repo is here:
https://github.com/Rasbats/opencpn-plugins-manual
You can see the docs folder.
github.io pages are here:
https://rasbats.github.io/opencpn-plugins-manual/opencpn-plugins-manual/0.1/index.html
Watchdog is one of the plugins created by using the module in your repo.
The oplaydo account is being used for my PR to developers to avoid having their forks in my Rasbats account.

Thanks for the links. As I understand it, an open source user is allowed just one github.io page? Is that correct?
Wow, you've done most of them! This gets complicated doesn't it.

Can a user web page support the individual plugin repositories documentation too?

"Can a user web page support the individual plugin repositories documentation too?"
Of course. Just copy the docs folder for the plugin to your site (or the contents).

Interesting points for the OPM here:
https://github.com/Rasbats/opencpn-plugins-manual/issues/1

Should a developer be able to use the plugin version as the version number shown for the manual for the plugin?
I think this is a good idea. Just needs a small change in antora.yml for the plugin, made by the developer(s).

I think this is a good idea. Just needs a small change in antora.yml for the plugin, made by the developer(s).

It might be better to generate antora.yml from antora.yml.in in cmake...
I would prefer to keep antora.yml in the module. CMake could update antora.yml in the manual location? This makes it possible for the developer to use other tools within the module e.g. pdf creation.
Agreed, that's what I meant, sorry if being too short

"I would prefer to keep antora.yml in the module"
I meant dev plugin component!

New manual build.
Summary:
Rick is now using mast-reorg for his updated watchdog manual. Change build.yml to reflect change.
Dashboard_Tactics accepted PR for the manual and is now a component. Update plugins.adoc. Remove module from the master repo modules.
Push to origin with .gitignore docs.
Test build locally.
blank .gitignore
Push to origin
Propose using beta for the version number for the beta plugins?

Mike, I don't know how this is going to work. I was in the middle of working on watchdog improvements, the manual came with me when I checked out mast-reorg, I tried to make the changes in master, but was taken back to mast-reorg so I ran antora bldloc.yml from that. What is the right way to do this? It should be coming from the master branch I think. When I commit I will run antora bldloc.yml again? There is some complexity here and we need a process explanation.

This was because you moved the manual to mast-reorg ;) You may want to go forward with the master branch. Move/use the manual there and I will edit my work to use that branch. To avoid confusion you need to stop using bldloc.yml. Get the result from Firefox or wait for the opencpn plugins manual build.

I didn't move it. When I git checkout -b mast-reorg it all came over to that branch. I was in the middle of that work and had to make changes to Watchdog as you wanted. I realize now that I don't generate for the Plugin Manual, that is initiated by you and done by antora from your repos for ocpn-plugins-manual

I find I have to see the results when I am editing. What is the problem with running bldloc.yml? Is it doing somethiing remotely too?
I don't think it should affect your antora build.yml?

I think it is my pushs of changes up to branch mast-reorg that is possibly causing something to happen there.

This is getting pretty complicated, I hope this will work in real life for Plugin Devs many of whom do not understand git as much.

"initiated by you and done by antora from your repos for ocpn-plugins-manual"
initiated by me but the antora.yml is the one in manual/watchdog that is used. My antora.yml is for modules not yet given to the devs.
"What is the problem with running bldloc.yml?"
No problem. It does not affect the build of the plugins manual.
"This is getting pretty complicated"
Agreed. But once you crack it you will see the advantages. Antora is well documented unlike my stuff !
Do you want to use the master branch of Watchdog?

Yes that is the main branch. I make checkouts for changes then merge back. Sean has not bothered to accept my pr's but I will try again in a little while. It is pretty clear he does not want to be bothered with the cloud overhead process. I will just continue with his other plugins as before.
Let me know when you are ready and I wil get things set up for another manual build.
OPM updated to include the change to Watchdog master branch. Radar_pi also updated with Hakan changes.

Does not look like I can stop the builds triggered by my PR for CircleCI.
https://circleci.com/docs/2.0/skip-build/
indeed, for some reason (why?) But this is only about the PR, not about your forked repo which is the main usecase. Again, just trying to read the docs.

Mockup for the betas:
https://rasbats.github.io/opencpn-plugins-manual/opencpn-plugins-manual/0.1/index.html
Use the version number to indicate a beta? (beta)
Take your point about moving the modules into components to make the left side index. At the moment it is easier for my work with devs to keep it as it is. Easier to find/move stuff. May need a new component for the betas, again with modules for each plugin, to start with.

looks great overall. Perhaps too early fir this kind of feedback, but I miss the short introductions: What is this User Plugins Manual, what is Beta Plugins? A few sentences on these subjects should be good.

For combining the three manuals this might be relevant:
https://gitlab.com/djencks/simple-examples/-/issues/2#note_537076523
It might need a conventional web page as Kathi already has for OpenCPN.org manuals.

Eventually, we need some decision-making here. One route would be to merge everything in ocpn-manuals or some other repo. Doing so, we would need to work with the structure and the TOC, but would stick to the Antora overall ideas. The different manuals would just be items in the overall TOC.

Another would be to keep the current "different manuals" approach where each manual a separate playbook.

Thoughts?

" One route would be to merge everything in ocpn-manuals or some other repo"
I think this might be difficult to structure, in view of the note above.
The plugins part would probably need a regular refresh for new material, whereas the other manuals would change less rapidly.
Keeping three separate manuals would make reporting issues and forking to allow PR easier.
Easy enough to make http links between the manuals. You already have this idea being used in Resources. The UI would be consistent across the manuals because of the common UI bundle. As well as Resources have a header item dropdown, OpenCPN Manuals.
I feel the separate approach is better.

The overall links are simple. What doesn't work that well is pointers to a specific part of another manual, right? Like a pointer from Developer Manual to the "Welcome to the community" part of the User's Manual etc.

As for the updates, I don't really see the problem. Why not rebuild the complete manual on a regular basis, each night or so? If/when there is a need to freeze a version, there manuals are versioned so we should be able to do that by freezing a given version and continue to work with next version (?)
Note: The manuals are still separate as seen when doing for example a PR. It's just about building all of them from the same playbook.
But there are separate components in different repos, which was why I raised the issue with David Jencks.

Hyperlinks are trivial, pointing to specific sections of another manual:
Added this to the plugins manual start page:
xref:betaplugins.adoc[Beta Plugins]
Test link:
https://leamas.github.io/ocpn-manuals/plugin-installer/0.0.1/Program-design.html#_catalog_and_tarballs
I still don't understand. Yes, there are multiple components in different repos, agreed. But I don't see the problem building all of them in the same playbook. What am I missing?

Your playbook would use antora.yml from the different manual repos as sources. The source for a manual is drawn from many components and you would not find all those components, I think.
Only the site.yml of the separate manual knows where all those components are and is able to build from them.
You can have many components in a single repo but not many repo with many components with a single playbook.yml to build them, I think. I could be wrong!

Problem is perhaps the notion of "my" playbook vs "yours"... What if ocpn-manuals was "our" playbook? Which we both have full access to? What would then be the problem to replicate the info from "your "site.yml into "our" playbook?

I did some basic tests and failed. But why not try replicating "my" site.yml in https://github.com/leamas/ocpn-manuals/
and see what happens? Will the references (page-id?)work across repos?
I may need to remove site.yml for this to be tested.
I will, but later. Preparing for work tomorrow for now. Stay tuned...

https://rasbats.github.io/opencpn-plugins-manual/opencpn-beta-plugins/0.1/beta-plugins.html
Used two components, one for the master plugins and the other for the betas. Propose using 'beta' for the version string for the beta to make them obvious. Both components in the same repo ;) Now to start delegating the modules.

I think we have confidence that Antora/asciidoc will be able to produce the updated manual for OpenCPN. Priority for me now is having a repo under the OpenCPN github repos. Without this it looks as if 'Rasbats' is taking over the world. Having "opencpn/opencpn-plugins-manual" would promote ownership of the plugin manuals.
In the mean time intend adding pages to explain the use of Antora/asciidoc, to help developers write/update their manuals. The wiki will form the basis for this.

https://rasbats.github.io/opencpn-plugins-manual/opencpn-master-plugins/0.1/index.html
Seeing the result perhaps 'Authoring the manuals' would be better in the dev-manual.
Thoughts?

I did a preliminary run with all components included in ocpn-manuals. Just looks fine, but we need to merge nav.adoc. While basically trivial, I have a open discussion with Rick about it, so we need to sit back here a little.

I agree that we have faith in Antora doing the job for the Developers and the Plugin manual. I also agree that we need to have this under the OpenCPN umbrella, but perhaps not yet. There are things to flesh out, like the structure of the DM and also the overall relationship between the manuals. So, perhaps continue in "local mode" for a while.
We clashed! 'Authoring the manuals'?
hm... "bewildered"... could you please expand a little...

In the wiki for the opencpn-plugins-manual I have some notes aimed at developers authoring plugin manuals.
https://github.com/Rasbats/opencpn-plugins-manual/wiki
This needs easy reference and my first thought was pages in the plugins manual.
https://rasbats.github.io/opencpn-plugins-manual/opencpn-master-plugins/0.1/index.html
But this could distract a user from searching for a plugin manual and perhaps the information needs to be in the developers manual.
Off to pack the car with boat bits. BBL
There already is a "Maintenance of this Manual" chapter in the DM , it fits nicely there. Ergo: we should make a common playbook which includes all the repos (DM + plugins) with a common nav.adoc.

@Mike Rossiter I have invited you as a collaborator in ocpn-manuals. Here is a branch called 'merge' which is the playground -- feel free to try whatever ideas you have. But please leave the main branch for now, to avoid pulling the carpet under Rick

In the merge branch, I have a first draft of a variant which merges all three manuals into one. It's far from perfect, bit looks in my eyes promising. I have not exported it, but 'antora site.yml' builds it in docs/ for preview..

In particular, see how the actual contents in each manual is delegated to "sub-nav" file in partials/. As I understand it, this could be part of another repo as well so for example the plugins contents could be maintained by opencpn-user-manual.
Accepted the invitation but I am unable to clone a fork of ocpn-manuals that I made in Rasbats. Is this because I am collaborating in leamas/ocpn-manuals?

My turn to be confused. I thought the discussion was about referencing adoc material in another component's adoc file. I don't see how we have moved forward with that. Yes, it is nice to see all the repos gathered together but this could have been done with a single web page using 2 repos? We now have 3 repos to get that result.
BUT you can build the website html in one place, which is a big step forward for maintenance.

I think we are getting sources and components mixed up. You are using the repo as a source. That source has many components. In dev-manual for example Rick cannot reference the manual-beta component plugins-beta.adoc page in plugins-manual, other than by using a hyperlink.
Another thing! Does the build of ocpn-manuals result in a build of ocpn-dev-manual for instance? Or is it just gathered as a source?
Here the idea is that that we view all manuals as a single "OpenCPN manual", where each manual is a subsection. Remember, just trying to explore paths here...
Since everything now is built in one go, for example rick is free to reference whatever he wants in the plugin manuals. Basically, everything is visible everywhere. This is IMHO a major advantage.

I get your message of the different roles. Have been very conservative with adding contents to ocpn-manuals, it's only a welcome page + navigation, nothing more. And, the core navigation files which lives under partials/ could and probably should be moved to plugins-manual and development manual. That is, if we want to explore this path.
"Cannot clone"... have you tried cloning into an alternative directory, like git clone git@github.com:leamas/ocpn-manuals.git foobar

"rick is free to reference whatever he wants in the plugin manuals"
Can we agree that this has to be a hyperlink reference not a component link?
"everything is visible everywhere. This is IMHO a major advantage"
Agree.
"Does the build of ocpn-manuals result in a build of ocpn-dev-manual?"
Can we agree that there is no build of the dev-manual? It is acting as a source.
"Can we agree that this has to be a hyperlink reference not a component link?" No. Why can't he just make a component link in this setup?
"Can we agree that there is no build of the dev-manual? It is acting as a source." For better or worse: yes.

"BUT you can build the website html in one place, which is a big step forward for maintenance"
Maybe we could take it from this point. If we want this to work, the only change required in the dev and plugins manuals is about navigation. Basically, it needs to be refactored with the main part living as a list in partials/. This would work just fine also in the local build, but also makes it possible to include it as a part of the overall nav.doc. Worth trying?
I cannot make the component link work myself, as discussed above. If Rick or yourself have a way of doing this I will update David Jenks with the result.
But if a make a PR?
Yes, that works but I wanted a clone in my github to play with.

"the only change required in the dev and plugins manuals is about navigation. Basically, it needs to be refactored with the main part living as a list in partials/."
I will have a play with this. Need to do some reading.
You still cannot clone? What is the error message?
I am using github desktop. Maybe that's the limitation. Have to make a food order. BBL
ON my way to the boat. Let's focus on the cloning first.... BBL

" have you tried cloning into an alternative directory, like git clone git@github.com:leamas/ocpn-manuals.git foobar"
Ok. With the command line cloning my fork of ocpn-manuals worked.
Various magic happened and I can now play with my fork of ocpn-manuals, without making changes to the leamas repo.
There has been so many attempts to create a git GUI wrapper. They all seem to fail as soon as there is something just slightly beside the standard workflow :) In the end, the CLI interface prevails.
In for an hour or so. Then, bicycle to the boat -- here is really nice weather
Thanks for the steer on partials. Improves the navigation considerably.
Now using this in plugins-manual.
Out of time now, but will look into it later. Please don't forget to push what you have :)
BTW: https://docs.antora.org/antora/2.3/navigation/include-lists/
Have updated ocpn-manuals (the merge branch) so it uses your partials instead of mine. This is IMHO a great step forward, manual can be built all at once or separately.

Now, going back to old issues:
"Rick is free to reference whatever he wants in the plugin manuals"
Can we agree that this has to be a hyperlink reference not a component link?

What am I missing here? Why cannot Rick use a component link if we build everything in one go a là ocpn-manuals?

The plugins manual now has two components - manual, with an id of "opencpn-master-plugins" and manual-beta with an id "opencpn-beta-plugins".
In the index.adoc for the manual-beta component I can use "xref:opencpn-master-plugins::index.adoc[Pages for the Master Plugins]" to make the link to the manual component, with the master plugins.
The ids are set up in two antora.yml one in the manual component and one in the manual-beta component. How does Rick reference "xref:opencpn-master-plugins" from the ocpn-dev-manual repository? I have tried and failed and, perhaps naively, believe David Jencks, one of the main Antora people, when he says it is not possible to reference a component outside the repo you are working in. Actually this is probably a good idea because you avoid broken links in repos outside your control.
The only way this would work would be to have a unified repo containing components (directories) for user manual, dev manual and plugins manual, all in the same repository. IMHO this would be a nightmare to maintain.
Perhaps I was naive... Give me some time to check...

"The only way this would work would be to have a unified repo containing components (directories) for user manual, dev manual and plugins manual, all in the same repository. IMHO this would be a nightmare to maintain."

Agreed
But still: give me some time...

I have now made a test:

  • Changed site.yml so it uses https://github.com/leamas/ocpn-dev-manual.git instead of https://github.com/rgleason/ocpn-dev-manual.
  • In ocpn-dev-manual I have made a component link like xref:opencpn-master-plugins:squiddio:squiddio.adoc[Squiddio] into the opencpn-plugins-manual

The test is pushed to the merge branch and it works just fine. So, it seems possible to make a component link from "Rick's manual" to "your manual" as I presumed. So, still feeling a bit dumb: What am I missing? And, why doesn't it work for you?
Interesting. Squiddio is not yet hosted by Mauro. Could you try, say, ShipDriver, which I am managing?
Other things in between... wait
A lińk to Shipdriver or from Shipdriver?

A link to ShipDriver from ocpn-dev-manual. xref:opencpn-master-plugins may not work because ShipDriver is an independent source. You would need to include it as a source in the dev-manual, as you did before, as an example.

It works. The merge branch builds the complete thing, using the linktest branch of https://github.com/leamas/shipdriver_pi.git instead of https://github.com/Rasbats/shipdriver_pi.git. See the commits for details

" I have tried and failed and, perhaps naively, believe David Jencks, one of the main Antora people, when he says it is not possible to reference a component outside the repo you are working in."

Can you link to this?

https://gitlab.com/djencks/simple-examples/-/issues/2#note_537076523
Running out of time this morning. I will have a closer look tonight.
OK, same for me. Thanks for the link. BBL
I'm a sloppy reader , again. The test is about a link from shipdriver to ocpn-dev-manual.
Added link dev manual -> shipdriver, in the community page. Also this one works.

Reading the docs, I find no limitations as to component links crossing git repositories boundaries. The only thing I see is basically a linker, and linkers are all the same: They first scan the input files to find all symbols (here, link targets) and builds a table with those. In next step, it re-scans the input files and resolves all the references (the xref's) using the table.

No, I havn't checked the code. It's just my mental picture how it works. Given that picture, I see no reason whatsoever why there should be any limitations of the kind we discuss.

"Added link dev manual -> shipdriver, in the community page"
Getting a 404 looking for community.adoc.
Could you give a link for this?

Not really a link right now. Can you

  • In ocpn-manuals, pull current merge branch tip at c834eea0f
  • Rebuild: antora site.yml
  • Browse: firefox docs/index.html
  • In the left navigation pane you find the community page: OpenCPN Developer Manual | Introduction | Community
  • The shipdriver link is in the line with a FIXME (second paragraph fir me)

Let me know if it doesn't work!
Shortcut to community page: firefox docs/ocpn-dev-manual/5.3.1/intro-Community.html

Sorry, you are linking in the same way that I use to build the plugins-manual. You are not linking to a component in opencpn-plugins-manual. You setup the source and use the antora.yml from the source. The source is not a component. If it was a component the xref would be something like "component::shipdriver:ROOT:shipdriver.adoc". (Note the double colon).
Your test is ok for just one plugin, shipdriver. shipdriver.adoc is generating the index page. If shipdriver had a further page, say demo.adoc the xref:shipdriver:ROOT:demo.adoc reference would not work because the antora in shipdriver points to shipdriver.adoc. I stand to be corrected!
One example is in Shipdriver... If you follow the link to "my" shipdriver there is a link to Plugin Guidelines. This is a secondary page in the Development Manual. or?
BTW: Why do you think that shipdriver isn't a component? As I see it, it is.

Hm... perhaps you got it wrong (?)... the shipdriver's antora does not really point to shipdriver.adoc. It's core thing is the name: shipdriver, which is used to resolve anything like shipdriver:ROOT:foo.adoc. The start_page: line is only used for the default page, the page used when the component is referenced without a page name. I. e., it can be dropped if there was an index.adoc, the default page name.
As an example, the ocpn-dev-manual's antora.yml doesn't have any start_page:. Still works just fine

https://docs.antora.org/antora/2.3/distributed-component-version/
Ok... perhaps we can describe shipdriver as a distributed component!
Where is this leading? Does the plugin manual have to build ocpn-dev-manual as a source just to reference the page "pm-plugin-guidelines.adoc". Why not a hyperlink? This is getting overcomplicated. I will sleep on it.

A hyperlink will only work at a specific location -> maintenance problems.

The plugin manual could either include ocpn-dev-manual, and have a working link to it. Or don't, in which case the link will be broken. It will then still work when built together with the others in ocpn-manuals.
Which means that the third option is to not include the dev manual as a source, but to build the whole set of manuals i. e., ocpn-manuals to see the "real" result.

Ok... perhaps we can describe shipdriver as a distributed component!

Preferably not. The link describes a distributed component as a single component with the sources in multiple git repos. However, shipdriver is not, it has all it's sources in a single repo. Ergo: It's just a component.

Fourth option:
Add the sources needed for explicit links to the dev-manual site.yml. This will be a limited number of plugins like testplugin, shipdriver plus ?
Make a simple start page like this one:
http://mikerossiter.co.uk/OpenCPN/opencpn-manuals/0.1/index.html
Why the complication of building the manuals together? I just don't get it.
We need to move this forward to allow plugin developers to see the results of their edits or interest will be lost.

@Rick Gleason
@Jon

Can we have your thoughts on this please?

Add the sources needed for explicit links to the dev-manual site.yml. This will be a limited number of plugins like testplugin, shipdriver plus ?

Works for links from dev-manual -> plugins, but not for links plugins -> dev manual
What is the reason for the link plugin -> dev manual?

We need to move this forward to allow plugin developers to see the results of their edits or interest will be lost.

Agreed. But to see the results, plugin devs can:

  • Build as today and, also as today, avoid linking to the dev manual.
  • Or, add links to the dev manual, clone the ocpn-manuals project and build that .

As long as there are no links plugins -> dev manual everything just works, be it a local plugin manual build or the overall ocpn-manuals. We have done the important step when modifýing the plugins and bera-plugins navigation using partials.
I am still totally against this monolithic behemoth that is ocpn-manuals.

So, there are three builds:

  • A single plugin manual.
  • The plugins manual or beta plugins manual
  • The ocpn-plugins-manual

Note that a plugin developer still needs to use another project to build the manual, usually opencpn-plugins-manual or, perhaps, ocpn-manuals.
And, to be fair, ocpn-manuals is certainly not monolithic. It's very much structured...
Let's sleep on it. The basic message is that we can build this thing either way. The only exception is when a plugin manual want to link to the the dev manual which at his point doesn't look common

The most common use case would be that the dev wants a help file for their plugin. The structure of the plugin manual is such that they can place a site.yml in their manual and build the html. Put the html in the plugin data folder and they have an offline help file that the plugin can use. otidalplan_pi has this.
Alternatively they can use some java and make a pdf for similar use.
And then there is just no sane way to have links to the dev manual...
This is offline stuff.
agreed
which means the all three builds (single plugin, all plugins or dev manual + all plugins) just works today. No changes needed
dev manual + all plugins???
I. e., ocpn-manuals

I have not digested this entire discussion and have been doing other things. Sorry about that. I believe that for ocpn-manuals I need to provide direct links to each repos. I've got to go now. Dentist. ....Later.

@Rick Gleason : You don't really need to spend any time to walk through this discussion. It's rather long, but does not boil down to any conclusions for ocpn-dev-manual or plain plugin manuals like watchdog. What has been done is modifying the opencpn-plugins-manual navigation.

We also have a branch in ocpn-manuals which builds all manuals, both ocpn-dev-manual and the plugins to a common document. It's yet unclear if or how this should be used.

Nothing of this affects ocpn-dev-manual. OTOH, this needs some love, there are open PRs etc.

Nothing to stop a repo having multiple folders each with an antora.yml and/or build.yml at it's head. So, what about asking Dave for a repo "manuals", under github.com/OpenCPN? Under this are folders "user", "dev", "plugins". Monster builds can take place using the top level manuals site.yml. This can be used to populate the OpenCPN.org website.
In, FI, plugins there is a build.yml that can be used to build test versions of the plugins-manual.
There can be an antora.yml in the top level of plugins to allow the site.yml to work. This can avoid using sources to pull in stuff.
@Alec : Could you setup a manuals repo with collaboration where we could test this arrangement?
The positives: True component references become possible. Less reliance on sources in other gits. Everybody can work in and test their own folders and contribute to other folders.
The negatives: Forking would result in a large download for a dev to use. However, once forked the dev could use just the plugins folder. A suitable .gitignore would avoid overwriting other manuals. Similar idea for a manual editor. Other negatives?
I sure could. However: What is the true advantages with this arrangement? That we all work in a common repo? This certainly comes with pros and cons (?)

True component references become possible.

Here, I don't follow you... In what way would a common repo support this better then the current ocpn-manuals?

Less reliance on sources in other gits.

Yes, this is a concern. All of a sudden the ocpn-manuals build breaks because of changes in the sources. More general, we don't have reproducible commits in ocnp-manuals, since the sources are not part of of the repo.

I have been thinking of using git submodules for this. That is, instead of a list of sources there is submodules, on for each source. This would be very similar to your approach, each module would be a subdirectory , but we could keep the current idea about different maintainers for different sources/submodules.

Pros:

  • Reproducible builds of ocpn-manuals
  • Distributed maintenance.

Cons:

  • complexity.
Thoughts?
One of my brilliant ideas which does not work. It turns out that antora doesn't support submodules. Back to the drawing board....

Sorry for late reply -- a lot of things happening for me now, not all of which related to opencpn.

This is great, it's the kind of experimenting we need to do before finding out the path to handle this. Some quick notes:

  • The build completes in a few seconds for me, hardly a monster. Overall, I don't find the size of the docs we produce overwhelming.
  • The possibility to create links are the same as when all repos are remote ones, like in ocpn-manuals
  • Having the main manuals in one single repo is a less complex setup, but needs clear informal rules about who owns what. The latter shouldn't be a problem.
  • Since the plugin manuals are remote (and has to be) we are still facing the problem that builds are not reproducible -- the same commit in manuals/ or ocpn-manuals/ can produce different output or even fail if a plugin repo is updated.

The solution to the last bullet was to use submodules -- but doesn't work. Same for git subtrees. I have an idea how to solve it by some scripting, but to little time.

'modules' folder in the manual repo (top level) was not needed... removed.

It doesn't really matter. It's a basic flaw in Antora: As soon as you have a remote r the build is non-reproducible. And it's sort of a blocking error for us as I see it.

see: https://antora.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/282400-users/topic/git.20submodule.20source.20problems/near/235687872

I have pushed a new branch manual to ocpn-manuals. It's a copy of rasbats/manual with a single commit on top.

That commit solves my concerns. It stores the plugins as locally cloned repos under a new directory sources. A new script source-state.sh handles this directory and a state file.

The state file lists all known plugin repos, their url and the commit used.

source-state.sh has three operations:

  • restore: restores all plugins sources using url and commit form state file
  • save: updates the state file with possible new repos + url and actual commit
  • update: Updates each plugin repo to latest HEAD.

Ideally, update should respect the branch setting in site.yml, but this requires python to parse the yaml. Perhaps later...

Since docs/ now can be generated from sources, it should really be added to .gitignore. Later, also this...

As soon as you have a remote r the build is non-reproducible. And it's sort of a blocking error for us as I see it.

Which means that the build is not reproducible. What is forged by site.yml depends on what's committed in each repo. Commits which cannot be controlled from the main manuals project. and then make a final build just to see it fail because of some commit in some plugin repo.

"Fail" in this context is not just about if the build fails -- in a way that would actually be lucky since it would be discovered. A much worse error would be if late commits introduced stuff which was wrong -- such errors would not be found.

And there is the general problem that a given commit in the main project does not reflect the actual state of the main manual. The build cannot be done at another time and produce the same result.

a given commit in the main project does not reflect the actual state of the main manual

We are talking here about a developer's plugin repo. Is this correct? Still getting my head around it.

The build cannot be done at another time and produce the same result.

Why does this matter?

No. rasbats/manual is currently at 4487d17. I could check out this very commit and build using site.yml. In a versiond world, this would give me the complete manual as of 4487d17. But realities are that the same commit will produce different versions of the manual(s), all depending on the state of the sources.

It matters a lot, because the whole idea with version management (not only git) is that a given version is inmutable. If not, there is no git diff, no possibility to make a patch and rebuild just adding the patch, and what not. Actually, it means that we have no version management of the overall manual(s)

Actually, it means that we have no version management of the overall manual(s)

Beyond my pay grade.

Each build could have a timestamp. Is this not sufficient? I have not seen this problem come up with other antora manuals?
No, theres is no version management. No patches, no history, no what not. We might as well stop using git for the playbook. And that's certainly not what we want.

Just consider the situation when an error is found and a patch is submitted (as a PR or whatever). We then have zero possibility to rebuild after just applying that parch and get the corresponding change. Instead, we will get a potentially new manual, with a need to check each and every bit instead of just checking what's patched.

Ok... following on from your idea of source-state could sparse-checkout help with speed and clutter avoidance:
source-sparse.sh
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/600079/how-do-i-clone-a-subdirectory-only-of-a-git-repository

Hm... interesting. Had no idea such a thing existed (hell, who has the complete knowledge what exists in git?). It looks like a solution which could be integrated into the source-state.sh script. And agreed, some optimization like that would be great (if this doesn't work, we could just remove everything besides manual/ after cloning)

So yes, some optimization is needed, and git sparse-checkout might be the solution.
One problem with sparse-checkout is that the configuration lives in the source. That is, for this to work we need to apply the sparse configuration to each and every plugin, and keep it sane. Doable?

As a first step I tried the dead-simple approach to just clone as usual, but remove everything besides manual/ and friends. The result lives in the branch state in ocpn-manuals. It brings down the sources directory to just over 400M. Good enough?
